ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for duck bamboo shoot vermicelli soup specifically; closest parent 'duck meat food product' (FOODON_03315595) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP 'Meat soups and broths' is the closest defensible parent category for this duck-based broth noodle soup; no specific duck vermicelli soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to duck bamboo shoot vermicelli soup as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of duck bamboo shoot vermicelli soup, approximately 450 g, comprising braised duck meat, bamboo shoots, rice vermicelli, and broth with fresh herbs and dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated by component analysis: duck meat (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 331960, 'Duck, domesticated, meat only, cooked, roasted'), rice vermicelli (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169726, 'Noodles, chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), dry'), bamboo shoots (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169226, 'Bamboo shoots, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt'), and broth/herbs estimated from USDA SR Legacy generic chicken broth as proxy for poultry broth. Values scaled to a mixed-dish composite per 100 g of finished soup.
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for duck bamboo shoot vermicelli soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for duck meat (cooked), rice vermicelli (dry, rehydrated weight adjusted), bamboo shoots (cooked), and poultry broth proxy. Approximate dish composition assumed: 25% duck meat, 20% rice vermicelli (cooked weight), 10% bamboo shoots, 40% broth, 5% herbs and sauce. Per-100g values derived from weighted average of components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli as analogous GI anchor (GI ~53, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for duck bamboo shoot vermicelli soup. The dominant glycemic carbohydrate source is rice vermicelli. Rice vermicelli GI is reported as approximately 53 (glucose reference) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The overall dish GI is estimated at 53 based on rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate driver; duck meat and bamboo shoots contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 45.9 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving at 10.2 g/100g) / 100 ≈ 24.3, rounded to 24.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth (~420 mg/100g; ~1890 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from the seasoned broth, which can contribute substantially to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming only half the broth is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli (~GL 24 per bowl) with beneficial protein and low sugar content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the moderate glycemic load contributed by the rice vermicelli portion; pairing with the duck protein and bamboo shoots may attenuate postprandial glucose response, but portion control of the vermicelli is recommended to manage blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from duck skin and cholesterol content, offset by lean protein and low sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request duck meat without skin to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake, and limit broth consumption to reduce sodium; the lean duck meat and bamboo shoots provide beneficial protein and dietary fibre with a relatively low saturated fat profile when skin is removed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Duck Bamboo Shoot Vermicelli Soup (鸭肉竹笋米粉汤; Bún Vịt Măng) is a Vietnamese-influenced noodle soup dish served in Singapore, most commonly found at Vietnamese restaurants such as Viet'Spice. The dish features tender braised or stewed duck meat, sliced bamboo shoots, and soft rice vermicelli noodles served in a rich, aromatic poultry broth. It is typically accompanied by fresh herbs such as Vietnamese coriander and mint, bean sprouts, and a dipping sauce such as ginger fish sauce or hoisin-chilli sauce. The broth is slow-cooked with aromatics including lemongrass, ginger, and star anise, giving it a complex, mildly spiced flavour profile. The texture contrast between the tender duck, crunchy bamboo shoots, and silky vermicelli is a defining characteristic of the dish. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from duck meat (approximately 8.5 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from rice vermicelli (approximately 10.2 g per 100 g), and relatively low fat when duck skin is removed. The sodium content is high due to the seasoned broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low GI) based on rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate source, with a moderate glycemic load per bowl. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for non-vegetarian diets. It is not reliably halal due to the use of duck and potential cross-contamination in restaurant settings. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its glycemic load, and high concern for hypertension due to broth sodium. It is a culturally significant dish representing the Vietnamese culinary tradition within Singapore's diverse food landscape.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for duck bamboo shoot vermicelli soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for duck meat (cooked), rice vermicelli, bamboo shoots, and poultry broth proxy. GI is estimated from rice vermicelli as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the component-estimation approach and the absence of a direct dish-level measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central approved sources; no direct dish-level measurement exists in any approved primary source. Values should be treated as indicative estimates pending a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the component sources used. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the component sources used. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components at the level of detail available. 5. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked duck meat and rice vermicelli per USDA FoodData Central; confirmed zero by component source data. 6. GI estimated at 53 based on rice vermicelli as the primary glycemic carbohydrate driver, sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. 7. The dish is served at Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt - Circular Road, Singapore, as recorded in field survey data (GD003457). 8. Halal status is not confirmed; duck meat sourcing and restaurant certification status are unknown. 9. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Vietnamese noodle soup bowl sizes in Singapore restaurant settings.
